conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 4 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 4 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 4.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> I am Player RED, and I am selling one unit of X. The production cost of X is 40 ZUP, so I need at least 40 ZUP to break even. I currently have 1 unit of X, and I want to sell it for a fair price. Since Player BLUE has ZUP to spend, I will propose a trade where Player BLUE gives me 40 ZUP in exchange for 1 X. This ensures I recover my production cost and possibly make a profit if the market allows. I will start with this proposal, and if rejected, I may adjust based on BLUE's response. </reason>
<player answer> Propose a trade with </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I am offering to sell one unit of X for 40 ZUP. This reflects the production cost of 40 ZUP per unit. If you accept, we both gain: I recover my cost, and you acquire the resource. Let me know if this works for you. </message>

<other player message> I think 30 ZUP is a fair price for the X. What do you think? </other player message>

<proposal count> 2 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE has proposed 30 ZUP for X, which is below my production cost of 40 ZUP. Accepting this would result in a loss of 10 ZUP. Since my goal is to sell the resource for at least enough to cover the cost, I cannot accept 30 ZUP. However, I am willing to negotiate to find a middle ground. I will propose 38 ZUP as a compromise—closer to my cost than 30, but still below my full cost to leave room for negotiation. This keeps the trade possible while protecting my minimum acceptable value. If rejected, I may consider further adjustments, but I will not go below 38 unless necessary. </reason>
<player answer> Propose a trade with </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 38 </newly proposed trade>
<message> I understand your offer of 30 ZUP, but it falls short of my production cost of 40 ZUP. I propose 38 ZUP as a compromise—close to my cost, but still allowing for a fair deal. This way, I make a small profit and you get the resource at a reduced price. Let’s see if this works for you. </message>

<other player message> I'm willing to offer 40 ZUP for the X. This seems like a fair price for both of us. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSE </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for ZUP. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>
<reason> Player BLUE has now proposed 40 ZUP, which exactly matches my production cost. This is the minimum acceptable price to avoid a loss. Since I have no incentive to sell below cost and no reason to reject a fair market price, accepting this trade is rational. The offer is fair: I recover my full cost, and BLUE acquires the resource at a price that reflects its production value. There is no benefit in further negotiation, especially since I’ve already made two proposals and this is the third. Accepting now ensures the transaction closes without risk of rejection. </reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message> I accept your offer of 40 ZUP for X. The trade is fair and reflects the production cost. Thank you for the agreement. </message>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 4 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 4 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 4.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```
